title: SEGMENT 12 — The haploid reference problem and the rise of variation studies

lines.text: SEGMENT 12 — The haploid reference problem and the rise of variation studies
I want to spend this segment on a foundational limitation of the 2003 reference genome that drove the entire next generation of human genomics projects.
The 2003 reference was, as I mentioned in Segment 9, a mosaic of about a dozen anonymous donors blended into a single haploid sequence.
Haploid means one copy of each chromosome — like reading half of your inherited DNA, with the other half ignored.

The reference also did not capture variation: it was one possible sequence, presented as if it were the human sequence, when in fact every actual human differs from every other actual human at millions of positions across the genome.
Here is the scale of human genetic variation.
On average, two unrelated humans differ from each other at roughly four to five million positions across the genome — that is about one variant per six hundred to seven hundred bases.
Most of these variants are single-nucleotide polymorphisms, SNPs, where one base substitutes for another.
About a million of the differences are insertions or deletions of one to fifty bases.
And then there are larger structural variants — duplications, inversions, copy-number changes — that can involve thousands or millions of bases at a time.
The 2003 reference captures none of this variation.
It is a single point in a multidimensional space of possible human genomes.

If you want to study disease — and remember, the long-term promise of HGP was that the genome would help us understand disease — you need to know not just the reference sequence, but the catalogue of variants and their frequencies in the population.
This problem was recognized from the start.
As early as 2002, while the finishing phase of the HGP was still ongoing, planning began for what became the International HapMap Project.

The HapMap Project, launched in 2002 and producing its main outputs in 2005 and 2007, was designed to catalogue common SNPs and the patterns of linkage disequilibrium — which variants co-occur on the same chromosomal segments — across major human populations.

HapMap genotyped about three million SNPs in samples from populations in Europe, Africa, East Asia, and later additional groups, and produced a public database of haplotype blocks — segments of the chromosome that are inherited as units.
The HapMap data made possible the first wave of genome-wide association studies, GWAS, which began in 2005 and 2006.
We will discuss GWAS more in Segment 17, when we talk about what HGP did not solve.
For now, the point is that HapMap was the bridge between the static 2003 reference and the dynamic study of human variation.

HapMap was followed by the 1000 Genomes Project, launched in 2008, which aimed to sequence the genomes of one thousand individuals — eventually expanded to about two thousand five hundred individuals — from twenty-six populations worldwide, at low coverage, with the goal of cataloguing essentially all human genetic variants with frequencies above one percent in any population.
The 1000 Genomes Project, completed in 2015, produced a catalogue of about eighty-eight million variants.
It was the first population-scale genome sequencing effort, and it transformed how human geneticists thought about variation.
Instead of asking "is this variant present in the reference," you could now ask "what is the frequency of this variant in Europeans, in Africans, in East Asians, in South Asians, in Americans?
" The 1000 Genomes data underlies essentially every modern GWAS imputation reference panel, every clinical variant interpretation pipeline, and every population genetic study of recent human history.
But even 1000 Genomes was not enough.
With twenty-five hundred samples, you could catalogue common variation well, but rare variation — variants present in less than one percent of any population — remained undersampled.
And the 1000 Genomes data was based on short-read sequencing, which missed many structural variants and could not resolve the difficult repetitive regions of the genome.
Two follow-up efforts addressed these limitations, and they bring us into the modern era.
The first is the Telomere-to-Telomere consortium, which finally completed the human genome in 2022.
The second is the Human Pangenome Reference Consortium, which in 2023 began replacing the single linear reference with a graph-based pangenome.
Those are the next two segments.
